title: Stable Homotopy without Homotopy
speaker: Toni Mikael Annala
abstract: Many cohomology theories in algebraic geometry, such as crystalline and syntomic cohomology, are not homotopy invariant. This is a shame, because it means that the stable motivic homotopy theory of Morel--Voevodsky cannot be employed in studying the deeper aspects of such theories, such as cohomology operations that act on the cohomology groups.

proof_stages.label: Representability as a way to compute operations
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from the principle that geometric representing objects can turn cohomology theories and their natural operations into computable homotopy or homology problems.

proof_stages.label: A1-homotopy and its p-primary limitation
proof_stages.summary: Morel-Voevodsky A1-homotopy theory is recalled as the algebro-geometric analogue of homotopy invariance, but it is inadequate for p-torsion or p-adic theories on characteristic-p or mixed-characteristic schemes.

proof_stages.label: Cohomology-operation motivation from resolution problems
proof_stages.summary: Topological bordism and spectral-sequence obstructions suggest that p-primary cohomology operations should detect failures of resolution or smooth alteration phenomena in algebraic geometry.

proof_stages.label: Projective formalism and P1-spectra
proof_stages.summary: The proposed replacement for A1-invariance is a category of sheaves of spectra on smooth S-schemes satisfying elementary blow-up excision, then stabilized by inverting pointed P1.

proof_stages.label: Basic outputs: K-theory and non-A1 MGL
proof_stages.summary: The framework is advertised as giving a novel characterization of algebraic K-theory and a universal oriented non-A1-invariant version of MGL, with some later discussion of endomorphism control and rational coefficients.

proof_stages.label: Twisted projective homotopy invariance
proof_stages.summary: The key theorem states that two sections of a vector bundle over a smooth scheme induce the same map in the P1-stable category after projective completion, with the proof sketched as reducing their difference to a commutator using the three defining properties.

proof_stages.label: Weighted A1-invariance from a stacky retraction
proof_stages.summary: Using a Zariski pushout of quotient stacks and the projective-homotopy theorem, the zero and unit points in A1/Gm are shown to become homotopic for every theory represented by a P1-spectrum.

proof_stages.label: Infinite excision and classifying-stack models
proof_stages.summary: An infinite-excision theorem for P1-spectrum-valued cohomology is combined with weighted A1-invariance to identify A∞/Gm with BGm and hence recover infinite projective space, and more generally vector-bundle stacks, as geometric models.

examples.name: Eilenberg-Mac Lane spaces representing cohomology
examples.note: The topological model K(A,n) shows that a geometric representing object turns cohomology operations into computable homology classes via Yoneda, motivating the search for analogous geometric control in algebraic geometry.
examples.engagement_reason: he uses the representing property of K(A,n) and Yoneda to identify cohomology of K(A,n) with natural cohomology operations

examples.name: Oriented bordism obstruction to resolving homology classes
examples.note: The manifold/bordism discussion uses the Atiyah-Hirzebruch spectral sequence as a topological analogy showing how homology operations can obstruct a singular geometric cycle from coming from a smooth manifold, thereby motivating analogous p-primary obstructions in algebraic geometry.
examples.engagement_reason: he develops the oriented bordism map to homology, invokes the spectral-sequence criterion, and explains how differentials obstruct resolving a homology class
